speaker: interviewer
text: Lilit Pohlmann in London, England. This is tape six.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And so, I ran and ran and ran. I didn't know where I was running to. I was just running. I-- [SIGHS] with the shopping, I can't remember did I drop the shopping, did I carry it. I really have a blank there. But I knew where I found myself eventually. It was a sort of a place in Lwow where people used to go picnicking a lot for weekends. And so it was like a small hill out of which, when you were-- when you were on it, you saw the panoramic view of the city, which was very beautiful. And it's a very well-known place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ran up there, all the way up there, and hid among trees and bushes and so on. And I just don't know. I mean, how come that nobody saw me, that there was no one there? It was in the middle of the week. All right, maybe people weren't picnicking, but somebody must have seen such a person crazy running. And there I sat for hour-- what seemed like hours, like hours, because I wasn't going to go back in the daylight. And I was waiting for night, really truly night, not just evening, night to fall.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was-- must have been summertime, because it was-- took a long, long time. And I was not dressed warmly, dressed in my [INAUDIBLE] dress. So, it was summer. And also, I had to see that I get back before the main door is shut. And that it at 10 o'clock. So, it wasn't, you know, really nighttime yet, but it was dark. And I began to make my descent. I have never seen this man afterwards. Of course, I never went-- never ventured out any more. That was my last escape. I mean, my last-- my last shopping trip, so to speak. But I understand later, on at some point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were told, Frau Wieth was told by the caretaker, or rather by his wife, one day she came home from work and the wife was-- the caretaker's wife was very weepy and looking very worn and distressed and distraught.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Frau Wieth said, well, what is the matter? What happened? Why are you-- she said, well, don't you know, my husband-- don't you know what's happening to my husband, and so on. She said, no, I don't. You know, what-- she says, well, don't you know that somebody saw a Jewish girl go in here? And the gestapo came in the middle of the night, took him away, and they're torturing him. Which was a horror. I mean, she came upstairs and told us this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were all-- I mean, I can't tell you. I cannot-- I have no words for that, but somebody must have seen me. Maybe then, at that time. Maybe at another occasion. I don't know. But somebody must have either known to me from before and recognized me, or assumed that I was a Jewish girl going in there. I don't know. And the gestapo came in the middle of the night, the way they used to do it. [SIGHS] But of course, they did not go to the other high eminence officers there. They went to the one and only non-German and non high official. And that was this poor caretaker. And they took him. And he had no idea. I mean, he was totally innocent. Totally innocent man. And they-- God knows, beat him up and got-- and they let him out eventually, but the feeling one had was absolutely terrible. [DRINKS] I never went out afterwards again. OK. But there was one more-- one more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adventure of sorts that I had while I was still out before. And that was I was walking the street, not where we were living, a little bit further. I don't know why I was going that way. Must have been that my shop didn't have what we wanted or what we needed, and I was going to the next best one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And suddenly, I had this feeling, this feeling that somebody is looking at me, somebody is observing me, you know? And I-- but I didn't-- I didn't want to start-- and then suddenly, in front of me, I don't know how many-- 15 meters or so in front, so like on the other side of the street, if you like, a Ukrainian militiaman standing there almost rubbing his hands, smug. With a smug smile on his face. He sees his victim.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] [MOANS] And-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: She can't do anything, I'm afraid. Carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: This militiaman, unfortunately, is-- was one-- was the-- dreaded, dreaded man in Lwow. He was bestial. I mean, he was the one who if, heaven help us, if anybody came into his hands. And he happened to have been the friend of our previous caretaker, the one who you'll remember was the one who denounced my father. And he used to come to him very often. And they both became immediately militiamen. And this one-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- knew me from before, because when we children played outside in the courtyard, so to speak, and they were-- he came to visit. I mean, he saw. He didn't know me. He even know me-- knew me by name, I think. If I'm-- if memory serves me, I think he even knew me by name. And suddenly now, he sees me from afar. And God knows how long he's been looking at me. He recognized me from afar. And I recognized him. I was in a terrible despair, and I thought, I can't do anything now. I mean, you know, these are flashes. These are moments which, when I tell you a story now, it's like I tell you it takes two minutes, one minute, five minutes, but this is a split second-- split-second decisions which you-- have nothing to do with logic whatsoever. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, I see him, and do I now turn around and run? Useless, because he'll catch me in no time. Walk on? He'll catch me anyway. So what do I do? Whichever I do, I'm a dead duck. I keep walking as I was, absolutely unperturbed. And I walk, and I walk straight at him, come up to him, and for no reason at all under the sun, in my most fluent and aggressive German, ask him for directions to some building, which was a German building called [NON-ENGLISH], which was not very far from there. I was in the right district for. If you asked me now to write-- write the story and figure this one out, I couldn't. Ever. But there I was in front of him, and this is what came to my mind. And at that moment, this man, his smugness, the smile was wiped off his face instantly. Sort of he was standing literally rubbing his hands. He just-- you know? Like a mummy. He was mummified. And at that moment, I said, don't you understand me? I raised my voice. And I was as Germanic as they could be. I said to him, well, what's the matter with you? I am in a hurry here, and I asked you a question. Can you direct me or not? He just-- nothing. I said, you know something, you people, you are something else. Here we are, we've been in this town now for what is it, a year, a year and a half? You haven't learned to understand one word we say to you. With what right do you wear your uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. I don't even know what I-- I said things to him, which-- that I remember saying. I said, and it's about time if you don't, then go and take somebody to teach you German. And in the meantime, there were a lot of people around us, because child shouting at such a-- and he-- not a sound. Not a word. Absolutely, you know, almost to attention. And I said, and goodbye. And I went off, you know, told him. And to the corner of the street, and then again did I start running.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was sure that he'll catch up with me, that he would-- but he must have been so stumped. He must have been totally-- he was like, you know, a pillar of salt standing there. He was motionless. And it was such a thing, when I think about it, when I remember this today, I have no idea how I did it. I don't know. It is incredible that one should have-- how-- what would you call it? I don't know. But it defies any logistics, anyway. Defies everything. But I only told you this because I didn't go out afterwards, after the German experience [NON-ENGLISH]. I never went out again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I must say that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going back, of course, yes, while I was still going out, there is one more incident when I was shopping there. There were a number of nannies coming with children. There were mothers coming shopping with their children and so forth. And occasionally, I saw-- and this woman saw me, she was coming with her children and saw me. I saw her, German. It was all right. We greeted each other, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: And on one occasion, she approached me and she said, oh, how very nice, you're a very nice girl shopping. Who do you shop for? So I didn't particularly want to-- I said, well, I shop for my family sometimes. My mother can't go out. She's not feeling very well, so I go out and shop. Ah, that's very nice, you're a good and nice girl. And to her children says, you see what a nice girl helping her mommy, you know, helping her mommy because her mommy is not very well, so she goes and shops for her. Would you do that, too? Would you? I don't think so. You know, that sort of normal talk. And I was very uneasy. She said, oh, you know, you have to come and have tea with us one day. I would love for you to come and be with my children. Such a nice girl. I said, yes, thank you very much, and oh, please, God, never, never. And so on one occasion some time later, again I meet her, and she says, oh, today could you come? And we're going home now, must you go home immediately? I said, well, really I ought to go home, but, you know, maybe I-- I could for just five minutes come in with you, or 10 minutes. You're very kind. I didn't want to antagonize or make her suspicious in any way, you know? I wanted to play natural. The child would like to when she is inv-- I said once I will ask my mommy if I may go when she once asked me, and I said yes, my mother said yes, I could. OK. So I said, that's very nice. Well, you can stay so long as you can, and then you can go home, or we can bring you home. No, no, I said, I live very near. So, OK. She was not insistent, and she was very pleasant. We come to a villa. Beautiful villa. Wonderful thing. And it's nice, it's summer, in the garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we're having our orangeade, I think, or something, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in walks a man. I mean, an SS general, you know, on his uniform he had it. And I saw him, and I really, really thought I felt faint, because he was the head of eliminations of Jews in Lwow, by the name of Katzmann. I mean, he was the fear. Just the name alone inspired every emotion in you of, you know-- this was a butcher. A very good-looking butcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was his house, and those were his children. How did I know? We didn't introduce ourselves. She didn't tell me. I mean, I was a child. Would she say my name is Frau Katzmann. No, she didn't ask me even my name. Nothing. She was just, oh, nice girl. That was that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He walked there. He kissed the children. He kissed his wife. He was, you know, a normal human being. For us, he was the executioner. And then he sort of-- and then after, he looked at me, and pointed and said-- meaning who is this, you know, with a smile. And his wife said, oh, this is this very nice girl I told you about. She comes shopping. Imagine, her mother is not well, and she comes and shops for her. And sometimes I meet her, and so I invited her to come play with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he said, oh, how very nice. Set himself down and took me by the hand, put me on his knee, and said, how very nice. You are a nice girl. What is your name? How old are you? You know. And I had to-- it was terrible. And I think about it now, really I-- I shudder, because I just don't know how I managed. And on two occasions before, he had my parents nearly at the death's door. He did. And there I am sitting on his lap, and he's nice to me, and talks to me normally and nicely, and sort of, you know, what a nice girl, yes, I heard about you, that's very nice, please come, you must come again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I must go now, because my mother is waiting. I really must go. I can't stay. But I will come again, if I may. Oh, he said, yes, you must come again. Anytime you like, please come. You know, things like this. Yes. It was-- it was unbelievable. Today, when I, you know, now sit and talk about it, it just doesn't seem believable to me, even. But it was. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I now go to the moment when my mother joined us at Frau Wieth. My mother was working in the ghetto-- in the ghetto-- for a marvelous, marvelous German man. He was a mini Schindler, except that he was, by character-- by character, not only because of the opportunity there and then, but generally, a truly magnificent man. Now, he was-- oh God, what is the qualification for a skin expert? A dermatologist.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, I don't mean-- I don't mean the human skin. I mean leather. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is-- there is a word for it. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Taxider-- not a taxidermist?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. No, no. Maybe I'll think about it later. At any event, he was a great specialist in leather. And from pre-war. And on the world scale, not just in Germany. He was an intern-- a man of international renown when it came to-- to hides and leathers and so on. And he had his factories and so on. And during the war, they sent him to Lemberg to open a factory in Lwow, Lemberg Lwow, to open a factory there to produce coats for the gestapo, their leather coats. A tanner. That's what he was, a tanner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this gentleman-- Herr Kohl was his name, like our present chancellor-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] arrived, came, looked. And he said yes, he will open the factory, but only inside the ghetto. Oh, they said, inside the ghetto? That's not on. He said, well, then in this case, I'm going back, I'm not-- my factory is inside the ghetto. Why do you want the factory inside the ghetto? He said because I want the best workmen. And the best workmen, the best tanners, the best that I know of are always Jewish people. And I want them to work for me. Oh, when they heard that, oh, that's all right, then. Fine. So, he opened that factory within the ghetto. And he employed approximately 40, between 40 and 50 people. I can't be quite precise.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] And my mother was one of them. In the ghetto, she was one of them. And they worked, they were doing the coats, those beautiful leather coats for the gestapo. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On one occasion, they came and told him that his people are now almost doomed. They're going for a selection, and he better look for a different place and so on, because this factory will be closed. These people are going to be taken away. He said, my people will not be taken away. Yes, they will. Of course, everybody shivered. Everybody knew that the moment they're dismissed, that means going for extermination. Not to a concentration, but extermination camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said, sit tight and don't worry. I'm going to discuss the matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in a horse and buggy, off he went. And it took hours. After some hours, they saw the horse and buggy, everybody shivering in their spine. Has he managed, has he not managed. And I should really stand up here to show you, but this camera won't allow it, right? So I have to do it sitting down. In the horse and buggy, he is standing up with a paper in his hand, waving to those poor people waiting there, glued by the windows, obviously, waving the paper that he got them off, that all is well. How did he do it? He bribed. Like Schindler, he bribed. He brought bales of the top, top magnificent leather, high-- you know, the finest for-- and bribed with that. This is all yours, plus the workmanship for your family, for your coats, for your children, for your grandmothers. But leave me my people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did. And they left those people. Of course, for the time being only. But that was the kind of man this was. Wonderful man. And he-- and I'll just say briefly here that at the end of the war, after the war, we found him, and we testified in Yad Vashem, and he was awarded the highest medal by Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kohl. Kohl. K-O-H-L, like the chancellor. And with the family, we are until today in touch. At any event, but that was only for a little while he managed this respite. And they were in remission, so to speak, poor things. And in May, at of the end of May 1943, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they burned the ghetto. They-- the ghetto was burning, and liquidating the ghetto. At that time, my mother had already lost her father, who was taken away from the ranks going to work. She had lost her father, and she still had her mother. And her mother was very ill then, but my mother wouldn't leave her. And my-- her mother was always asking her to leave her, to go to me and to be with me. But my mother wouldn't do that. And now, the ghetto is burning. And there they are. And my grandmother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] jumped from the third floor and committed suicide so that my mother may go to me. She told her, now you can go. And she wasn't even dead when she-- when she-- she was not quite dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When this happened. Sorry. [CRYING] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother asked the doctors would they look at her, and there was no-- there was no hope at all. There was none. And my mother then managed to escape from the ghetto, from the burning ghetto. She had just went out completely in her eyes, you know. Took the arm band off, and walked through town. And nobody stopped her. And she came to me, or rather, to ask where we were. [CRYING] And from May '43, we were together. And from May '43 until now, we are together. And she-- we were four people by that time. This poor Frau Wieth had four of us then. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [DRINKS] And we were sleeping on the floor in the kitchen. Every night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one morning very early, about six o'clock in the morning-- our sleep was not a very deep sleep. We were incredibly alert to every sound, to every slightest, slightest motion anywhere. And the kitchen was very near the entrance door. We sat and we heard definitely like somebody is by the door, like walking the steps and by the door. There was no need to walk the steps, because there was a lift. But there was somebody definitely walking the steps. It was by the door. Instantly, we cleared up ourselves off into the scullery. There was a scullery the size of-- I don't know, I mean-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six feet by six. I mean, tiny. Just to put a few things in-- in the kitchen. All of us into that scullery, and no sign of us. And within moments, well, as we hid in that scullery, we knew definitely that somebody was there. We didn't know who. [DRINKS]
